title: Vareniki
subtitle: Ukrainian potato and onion dumplings
excerpt: Boiled dumplings filled with mashed potato and caramelised onion, served with fried onion, soured cream, and dill. The Ukrainian equivalent of pierogi, slightly larger, with a softer dough, and deeply tied to national identity.
sourceNotes: Vareniki are one of the most important dishes in Ukrainian cuisine and a symbol of Ukrainian domestic cooking. Like Polish pierogi, they can be filled with potato, cheese, cherries, or sauerkraut, but the potato-and-onion version (vareniki z kartoplieyu) is the most widely eaten. The dough is typically softer and slightly thicker than pierogi dough, made with boiling water rather than egg and soured cream. The fried onion served on top is not optional. It is as much part of the dish as the dumpling itself.

content.text: Vareniki dough is made with boiling water instead of egg and soured cream. That gives a softer, more flexible result, a touch easier to seal than the Polish pierogi dough. The filling is plain: mashed potato with caramelised onion, seasoned well. The fried onion on top is what ties it together. Its sweetness and char against the plain potato is the flavour contrast that makes the dish work.
The dough should be worked quickly while the water is still warm. Resting it for 30 minutes is important, the gluten relaxes and the dough becomes much easier to roll.

For the dough: mix the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{sunflower-oil}} and 150 ml of boiling water. Mix quickly with a spoon, then knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes. For the filling: boil the {{potato}} in salted water until tender. Drain and mash. Cook the {{onion}} in the {{unsalted-butter}} until golden and caramelised, 12 to 15 minutes. Mix into the mash and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Cool completely.

Roll the dough on a floured surface to 3 mm thickness. Cut rounds using a 9 cm cutter. Place a generous teaspoon of filling in the centre, fold and press the edges firmly to seal. Meanwhile, fry the 2 sliced {{onion}} in the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium heat for 15 to 18 minutes until deeply golden. Boil the vareniki in batches in salted water. They are ready 3 minutes after floating. Remove with a slotted spoon and toss immediately in a little of the fried onion butter. Serve topped with the fried onion, {{soured-cream}}, and {{dill}}.

items.fix: The water must be boiling when added, this partially cooks the starch and makes the dough pliable. If stiff, rest a further 15 minutes wrapped in a warm cloth
items.cause: Not enough water, or dough not rested

items.fix: Cook in small batches and press each seal twice. The water should be at a rolling boil when the dumplings go in
items.cause: Seal not pressed firmly, or overcrowding the pot

items.fix: Mash the potato while still hot and add the butter immediately. A little soured cream stirred into the filling keeps it moist
items.cause: Potato over-dried before mashing, or too little butter in the filling

content.text: Vareniki are central to Ukrainian food culture in a way that goes past any recipe. They show up in stories, folklore, and the national mind as a symbol of home cooking and comfort. They are made at family gatherings, at Christmas (for the sweet cherry version), and on weeknights when something filling is needed. The fried onion on top is not a garnish but part of the dish. The caramelised sweetness against the plain potato and sour cream is the mix that defines the flavour.
